coredata.dc:title: Design and development of folate-chitosan/CD nanogel: An efficient fluorescent platform for Cancer-specific delivery of AntimiR-21
coredata.dc:creator: Radnia, Fatemeh
Mohajeri, Nasrin
Hashemi, Fatemeh
Imani, Mahsa
Zarghami, Nosratollah
coredata.dc:description: Advances in the field of simultaneous diagnosis and treatment of cancer have introduced “nanotheranostic” systems that enable targeted co-delivery of imaging probes and therapeutic compounds. In this study, a simply designed fluorescent construction has been developed as a novel antimiR-21 nanocarrier for targeted imaging of SKOV3 cells. Fluorescent green carbon dots (CD) were synthesized from grapefruit extract through hydrothermal reaction. Folic acid-chitosan (FA-CS) conjugate was prepared according to the covalent chemistry for simultaneous loading of CD and antimiR-21 sequence in order to design FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles. The FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles showed uniform size distribution of 104.9 ± 30.68 nm. Nanoparticles with negligible toxicity and good serum stability transferred antimiR-21 sequence to cancer cells with 80–90% entrapment efficiency. In vitro cellular imaging and distribution studies were investigated by fluorescence microscopy that revealed higher cellular uptake of FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel by SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells, which highly express folate receptors compared to fibroblast folate negative (FR−) (NIH3T3) cell lines. Ultimately, this investigation demonstrates that CD-loaded FA-CS nanogels provide a unique construction for cancer-specific fluorescent imaging capable of loading therapeutic agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green carbon dot
Folic acid
Targeted imaging
Chitosan nanogel
AntimiR-21

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of folic acid and chitosan (FA-CS) conjugate
Preparation of CD/antimiR-21 loaded hybrid nanogels
Characterization of the fluorescent hybrid nanocarrier and antimiR-21 loaded nanocarrier
Binding efficiency of antimiR-21 with fluorescent nanocarrier
Investigation of in vitro cellular uptake

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis and photoluminescence properties of NPs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CD, FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogel, and CS nanogel. Insets refer to photographs of aqueous dispersion of NPs under normal and UV light. (b) Photoluminescence emission wavelength shifts to longer wavelength (from 450 to 550 nm) as excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 420 nm.
The fluorescence intensity changes of FA-CS (CD/antimiR21) nanoparticles over 2 h exposure to a continuous excitation with λex = 360 nm.
FTIR spectra of pure chitosan (CS) (a) folic acid (FA) (b), and folic acid-chitosan conjugate (FA-CS) (c).

Morphological analysis of FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles (a) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles (b).
Particle size distribution of FA-CS(CD) and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs, measured by DLS and reported by number.
AntimiR-21 binding capability assay. Binding of antimiR-21 into the FA–CS(CD) nanoparticles (NPs) at various weight ratios of NPs to antimiR-21.
AntimiR-21 serum stability assay. The time-dependent degradation of nuclear acid exposed to 10% serum was observed for (a) naked antimiR-21 and the antimiR-21 in (b) FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanocomplex.

Particle size changes of nanoparticles stored at cool environment (4 °C) and ambient temperature measured at specific time intervals (0, 2, 5, 10, and 15 days). Nanoparticles were suspended in distilled water (pH in the range of 6–7).

In vitro bioimaging of cellular uptake. (a) Fluorescence microscopy imaging of treated SKOV-3 cells (FR-positive) with FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) and CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles. (b) Fluorescence microscopy imaging of treated NIH3T3 cells (FR-) with FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) and CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles. (c) The intracellular fluorescent nanoparticles were quantitatively analyzed by imageJ software.
Cytotoxic assay of SKOV3 cells after incubation with various concentrations of CS(CD), FA-CS(CD), and FA-CS (CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles at 48 h.
Nanoparticle size and zeta potential of FA-CS (CD) and FA-CS (CD/antimiR-21).

head.ce:title: Design and development of folate-chitosan/CD nanogel: An efficient fluorescent platform for Cancer-specific delivery of AntimiR-21

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advances in the field of simultaneous diagnosis and treatment of cancer have introduced “nanotheranostic” systems that enable targeted co-delivery of imaging probes and therapeutic compounds. In this study, a simply designed fluorescent construction has been developed as a novel antimiR-21 nanocarrier for targeted imaging of SKOV3 cells. Fluorescent green carbon dots (CD) were synthesized from grapefruit extract through hydrothermal reaction. Folic acid-chitosan (FA-CS) conjugate was prepared according to the covalent chemistry for simultaneous loading of CD and antimiR-21 sequence in order to design FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles. The FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles showed uniform size distribution of 104.9 ± 30.68 nm. Nanoparticles with negligible toxicity and good serum stability transferred antimiR-21 sequence to cancer cells with 80–90% entrapment efficiency. In vitro cellular imaging and distribution studies were investigated by fluorescence microscopy that revealed higher cellular uptake of FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel by SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells, which highly express folate receptors compared to fibroblast folate negative (FR−) (NIH3T3) cell lines. Ultimately, this investigation demonstrates that CD-loaded FA-CS nanogels provide a unique construction for cancer-specific fluorescent imaging capable of loading therapeutic agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging probes have been broadly studied as one of the most desirable nanomaterials in biosensing, bioimaging, cellular and biomedical researches in recent years [1–3]. Despite the considerable progress in diagnostic and therapeutic approaches [4], cancer still remains to be one of the leading causes of death worldwide. Considering various side effects of the current cancer treatments like chemotherapy, generating a combined platform capable of cell targeting and imaging both in a single paradigm would provide a novel approach with greater therapeutic efficiency. Therefore, targeted imaging capability can facilitate precise identification of tumor sites and monitoring of the treatment responses [5–7].

Primarily, fluorescent nanoparticles including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) of metallic elements have been widely studied in the last two decades [8]. Encapsulation of QDs with natural biopolymers was considered to decrease their cytotoxicity for in vivo applications. Nevertheless, the ultimate release of toxic metals into the biological system is inevitable [9,10]. In order to address this problem, CDs as the most recent invention of carbon nanomaterials with several advantages including excellent multicolor photoluminescence properties, low toxicity, water solubility, and several functional groups have been greatly investigated [11]. However, the necessity for chemical carbon sources, toxic materials (strong acid/alkali), special equipment, and stabilizing agents with high costs still remains the major challenge in either top-down and bottom-up methods of CD synthesis. In this regard, green synthesis approaches based on the use of natural carbon sources has gained growing popularity as an ecofriendly and affordable method of CD synthesis termed “green CDs” [12].

With the advent of green-CDs, a large number of studies have focused on the nontoxic nature, high efficiency, and application of green-CDs in bioimaging [13], biosensing [14], and cancer diagnostics [15]. Besides, capsulation of CDs with biopolymers has increased their stability and circulation time by preventing systematic degradation which promises the development of effective image-guided drug delivery systems [16,17].

Image-guided gene delivery with a single nano-platform has provided non-invasive diagnosis and treatment of various abnormalities, including genetic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, and cancer [18,19]. Antisense oligonucleotides have been investigated as promising novel cancer treatments, due to their ability in regulating the expression of proteins and mRNAs associated with cancer specific signaling pathways [20–22]. However, negatively charged phosphate groups of the oligonucleotides prevent them from passing through the cell membrane, which makes them very susceptible to degradation and rapid renal clearance in vivo and in vitro [23]. In this regard, developing suitable materials to facilitate gene transfer within the target cells is mainly challenging [24].

Among the natural biopolymers, chitosan has been extensively investigated and optimized for gene delivery due to its high biocompatibility, bioadhesion, and ability of forming stable polymer/DNA complexes. Chitosan is composed of N-Acetyl-D-glucosamine and D-glucosamine units and made from the deacetylation of chitin. In addition, among all other biodegradable polymers, represents cationic properties, enhanced cellular permeation, and the potential of making ionic interactions with negatively charged molecules. Given its pKa is less than physiological pH, chitosan polymer is amenable to enhance endosomal escape for cargo delivery [25,26].

Targeted delivery of therapeutic nanocarriers to tumor cells through ligand-receptor interaction is a desirable possibility to accomplish efficient treatment and prevent side effects. The folate receptor as a convenient target for cellular imaging and cancer therapy is commonly over-expressed in a variety of human cancerous cells. While folate receptor is less distributed in healthy tissues and organs [27]. Moreover, folate-conjugates are more likely to enter cells through folate receptors than free folate, which makes them selectively desirable for cancer cell detection [28]. The preference for folic acid-modified chitosan (FA-CS) as a polymer for incorporating CDs and genes was due to its biocompatibility and feasibility of excessive modification of functional groups.

Up to now, there has been no detailed investigation on the synthesis of green-CDs embedded in chitosan nanogels. To develop the biocompatible fluorescent nanoparticles as promising theranostic agents, encapsulating green CDs in chitosan decorated with folate targeting ligand would assure for selective targeting of tumor cells. In the present research, the main goal is to evaluate the efficiency of folate-conjugated chitosan hybrid nanogels as a novel nanocarrier of both green CDs and antimiR-21 in order to sustained imaging of targeted delivery of antimiR-21 antisense sequence to SKOV3 cancer cells. Initially, the fluorescence properties, serum stability, and cytotoxicity of NPs were investigated. Further, cancer-targeting effect and bioimaging application of the designed platform were thoroughly investigated with CS (CD/antimiR-21) and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogels in SKOV3 as a folate receptor positive cancer cell and NIH3T3 as a folate receptor negative cell line.

Chitosan powder (low molecular weight, degree of deacetylation ≥75%), Folic acid, Ethyl-(3-3-dimethylam-inopropyl) carbondiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxy-succinimide (NHS), anhydrous dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo(−z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT), penta‑sodium tripolyphosphate (PSTPP), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 cell culture medium were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Germany). AntimiR-21 was designed according to miRBase database and purchased from Macrogen (Seoul, South Korea). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), penicillin, and streptomycin were obtained from Merck Chemicals Co. The cell lines (NIH3T3, SKOV3) were supplied from the National Cell Bank of Iran (Pasteur Institute). Grapefruits were purchased from a local supermarket (Tabriz, Iran). All experiments were carried out using deionized water.

CDs were fabricated using a single-step hydrothermal reaction following previous studies [29,30]. Briefly, 20 mL of grapefruit juice was poured into foiled heat resistant bottles and then heated up to 160 °C for 4 h. After the samples cooled down at room temperature, the aqueous red-brown sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate the less-fluorescent remaining debris. Additionally, the supernatant solution was purified by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to separate the undesired larger products fallowed by adjusting pH to 5 using 1 M NaOH. The CDs were purified by dialysis against deionized water using a dialysis membrane (100 MWCO) for 24 h. Subsequently, CDs were powdered through lyophilization. The prepared CDs could be stored for three months in a dark place at 4 °C for future desired applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of folic acid and chitosan (FA-CS) conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The process of FA-CS conjugate preparation based on the following protocol [23]: A mixture of folic acid (44.1 mg), EDC, and NHS in anhydrous DMSO (the molar ratio was respectively 1:1.5:1.5) solution was prepared and stirred until folate was totally dissolved. The mixture was then added slowly into a 0.5% (w/v) chitosan solution in 1% acetic acid with stirring while both solutions were at pH 5.0. The mixture was stirred at the room temperature avoided from light for 16 h. The resulting mixture solution was adjusted to pH 9.0 through dropwise addition of 1.0 M NaOH. The yellow sediment was separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with NaHCO3 solution (pH = 8.5). In the following, the solution was dialyzed against water for 3 days using a dialysis tube MWCO = 14 kDa followed by subsequent lyophilization.

CD loaded CS/FA-CS hybrid nanogels were prepared by ionic gelation method between positively charged chitosan polymer and negatively charged TPP. Briefly, the process of synthesis is described as follows: 0.1% CS or FA-CS solution was prepared by dissolving 5 mg CS powder/FA-CS conjugated polymer in 5 mL acetic acid 4% solution with continuous stirring (1200 rpm) overnight. The solution pH was set at 5.5. 5 mg of CD powder was dissolved in 0.6 mL TPP aqueous solution (0.1%) with pH 8. The CS/FA-CS(CD) nanogels were spontaneously made via the dropwise addition of as-prepared CD/TPP solution to 2.4 mL of CS or FA-CS solution (chitosan to TPP weight ratio of 4:1) under continuous magnetic stirring (1000 rpm) avoided from light at room temperature for 2 h. The hybrid nanoparticles were congregated by centrifugation at 13,000 ×g for 15 min.

For the association of antimiR-21 with hybrid nanogels, 18 μL of antimiR-21 (1.3 μg/μL) in DEPC water was added to the TPP solution (0.6 mL, 0.1%) containing 5 mg CD, before the drop-wise addition to the CS or FA-CS solution (2.4 mL, 1 mg/mL) under constant magnetic stirring in a dark place to create CS(CD/antimiR-21) and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) respectively.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of CD, CS, FA, FA-CS conjugate were recorded by FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) using KBr pellets at the room-temperature, and the spectra was calculated from 4000 to 500 cm−1 at 4 cm−1 spectral resolution. To confirm the formation of nanoparticles, the morphology and size of the nanoparticles were analyzed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) system (FE-SEM TESCAN MIRA3). The morphological analysis of FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogel also was performed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The particle size distributions and zeta potential were characterized using laser dynamic light scattering technique (DLS) (Zetasizer-ZS90, Malvern, UK) in the water at 25 °C. Also, the presence of CDs doped into the chitosan nanoparticles was confirmed using (Cytation5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader, BIOTEK) to evaluate the UV absorbance and fluorescence intensity.

The efficiency of antimiR-21 entrapment into the FA-CS(CD) constructs was evaluated using the Thermo Scientific™ NanoDrop UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific™, England) at 260 nm. In brief, the concentration of free antimiR-21 in the supernatant recovered from particle centrifugation (13,000 ×g, 30 min) was quantified by absorbance measurement at 260 nm. The supernatant obtained from unloaded FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles (without antimiR) was used as a blank.
Encapsulation efficiency =
W
sample ▬ W
supernatant
/ W
sample
× 100
where W sample is the weight of antimiR added and W supernatant is the weight of antimiR in the supernatant.

Efficiency of antimiR-21 binding to FA-CS(CD) nanogel was measured by agarose gel electrophoresis. Different ratios of NP to antimiR-21 (w/w) including 20:1, 30:1, 50:1, 75:1, 100:1, and 150:1 were prepared. Then the hybrid nanoparticle samples were electrophoresed on a 4% agarose gel containing 10× TAE buffer (Tris 400 mM /Acetate 200 mM/ EDTA 10 mM) and pre-stained with safe stain at 110 V for 60 min. The naked antimiR-21 was used as a reference. The oligonucleotide retardation was visualized with a gel documentation system.

The stability of naked antimiR-21 and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles against serum digestion was investigated. In addition, to evaluate their stability in the cell culture medium, FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles and naked antimiR-21 were incubated in an equal volume of RPMI 1640 culture medium supplemented with 10% FBS for 0 min, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h. At each time interval, 50 μL of the mixture was separated and kept at −20 °C to be prepared for gel electrophoresis. The free antimiR-21 was used as an internal control. In order to stop serum activity before running the gel electrophoresis, the samples were incubated in an incubator bath at 50 °C for 4 min. To separate antimiR-21 from the nanoparticles, 5 μL of heparin (1000 U/mL) was then added. Finally, treated samples were loaded onto a 4% agarose gel stained with safe stain for electrophoresis at 100 V for 60 min.

Prepared FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles were centrifuged at 13,000 ×g for 15 min and the obtained pellets were resuspended in deionized distilled water (pH in the range of 6–7) to be stored at 4 °C and ambient temperature for 15 days. To evaluate the stability of the NPs during storage, size distribution of the particle was estimated at the determined time points (0, 2, 5, 10, and 15 days) of storage.

The cytotoxicity of CS(CD), FA-CS(CD), and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles on SKOV3 cells was analyzed by MTT assay. SKOV3 cells were cultured at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well in 96-well microplates. After overnight incubation at 37 °C under 5% CO2, the medium in each well was replaced with various concentrations (10, 20, 40, 80, and 160 μg/mL) of a fresh medium comprising CS(CD), FA-CS (CDs) and antimiR loaded nanoparticles and incubated for 48 h. The wells with no samples containing normal medium were used as control. The test and control solutions were replaced with MTT reagent (5 mg/mL in PBS) and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C to initiate the formazan formation. The reaction was hindered by replacing the medium with 200 μL of DMSO solution for cell lysis, and the optical density of dissolved formazan was recorded by a microplate reader (7250CE-7000 series, England) at 570 nm [1,31].

Cellular uptake and nanoparticle distribution were visualized under fluorescence microscopy. SKOV3 cells were seeded in 24-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 in RPMI 1640 and incubated for 24 h. Next, the cells were incubated for 2 h with CS(CD/antimiR-21) or FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanocomplexes (100 μg/mL) which was dissolved in RPMI 1640 containing 10% FBS. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4) to take out excessive nanoparticles and then fixed with methanol solution for 15 min. The NP uptake was observed using fluorescence microscopy (Cytation5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader, BIOTEK) with GFP filter. To quantitatively determine the uptake of NPs into cells, the fluorescence intensity of each sample was examined. Moreover, to determinate the level of corrected total cellular fluorescence (CTCF) from fluorescence microscopy images, imageJ software was used by the following equation:
CTCF = Integrated Density −
Area of selected cell
X
Mean fluorescence of background readings

Despite significant advances in the context of early diagnosis and treatment of cancer, conventional cancer therapeutic methods still have remarkable challenges to assure efficient therapy. Nanotechnology provides a unique opportunity to develop novel, highly effective, and less invasive strategies for simultaneous diagnosis and treatment by providing a wide range of multifunctional nanoparticles [32]. Additionally, nanomaterials can be specifically targeted with ligands to cancer cells through ligand-receptor interaction that promises higher affinity to target sites while diminishing off-target cellular uptakes. Co-delivery of therapeutic materials and imaging agents within a single nanoplatform as a novel strategy has achieved massive popularity in cancer treatment by providing real-time screening of the disease site and therapeutic responses [33]. Recent advances in fluorescence imaging have simplified the accurate monitoring of endocytic pathways to reach a detailed understanding of intracellular drug delivery processes. Nowadays CDs are considered as interesting candidates for real-time detection of cellular components and therapeutic agents, that could be used in a single cell level [34]. To this end, this study aimed to assemble folate decorated chitosan hybrid nanogel co-delivering of green-CD and antimiR-21 that enabled cell-specific targeting along with fluorescence imaging to improve treatment efficiency.

Initially, FA-CS conjugates were prepared using EDC/NHS coupling reagent for covalent conjugation of carboxylic groups (–COOH) of FA and amino groups (−NH2) of chitosan. The application of chitosan as a biodegradable material for the protection and delivery of oligonucleotides in in vitro and in vivo systems was improved by FA conjugation as a targeting ligand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD/antimiR-21 loaded hybrid nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: FA-CS conjugates were self-assembled into hybrid nanogels through gelation method and combined with CD and antimiR-21 during the spontaneous formation of nanoparticles as FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21 nanoparticles). Indeed, we designed a functionalized fluorescent nanocarrier for antisense therapy by incorporating CDs from the cellular imaging perspective.

For this purpose, first green CDs were initially synthesized through the single-step hydrothermal method. Then, biocompatible folate-chitosan (CD/antimiR-21) nanogels were self-assembled through a two-step method using TPP as an anionic reagent for cross-linking of cationic chitosan/FA-chitosan polymer. Chitosan chains have amine groups (NH2) in repeating units that are positively charged in the mildly acidic medium of the synthesis reaction (pH 3.5–5.5). Therefore, chitosan is widely used as a delivering agent capable of entrapping negatively charged macromolecules like oligonucleotides. In addition, according to the FTIR results, CDs have a hydrophilic surface surrounded by OH / COOH / NH2 groups that account for CDs immobilization in chitosan by interacting with OH / -NH2 groups of chitosan polymers. On the other hand, the negative charge of the ionized CD might be partially contributed to the gelation by electrostatic interactions in this self-assembled nanostructure. Accordingly, CDs and antimiR are likely to form tight complexes with cationic polymer of chitosan in the synthesis pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fluorescent hybrid nanocarrier and antimiR-21 loaded nanocarrier
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogels were investigated and compared to CDs. The aqueous dispersion of FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogel in visible light was clear yellow and upon the irradiation of UV-light (365 nm), strong blue light was immediately generated. Fig. 1a compares the characteristic UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs, chitosan nanogels, and FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogels. Although there is no considerable absorption at the wavelength above 280 nm for chitosan nanogel, free CDs represent a distinct broad peak centered at 280 nm, which is attributed to the π - π⁎ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of CDs. In comparison to the chitosan nanogel and free CDs, the synthesized FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogel demonstrates a similar absorption peak at 280 nm referring to the successful immobilization of CDs into the FA-chitosan network.

FA-CS(CD) hybrid nanogel is expected to establish quite similar tunable photoluminescence properties to the free CDs, which also could be considered as the proof of successful embedment of CDs into the FA-CS network. The photoluminescence emission wavelength of the FA-CS(CD) nanogel shifts to longer wavelength (redshift) and the intensity decreases steadily over an excitation wavelength (λex) range of 340–460 nm, which might be affected by the several surface energy traps of the CDs. The keenest fluorescence happens at 450 nm excited at λex = 360 nm (Fig. 1b). The fluorescence quantum yield of the CD and FA-CS(CD) nanogel were determined to be 56% and 48% respectively, using quinine sulfate as a fluorescence standard. For loaded CD, the possible surface interaction with chitosan network leads to the decrease of CD quantum yield, which would affect the surface and optical properties of CD by quenching the emitted photons. Moreover, FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs exhibited excellent photostability. Fluorescence intensity change of nanoparticles was investigated during 2 h of continuous UV light excitation (λ ex = 360 nm). The maximum PL intensity gained at emission wavelength of 450 nm witnessed a slight change over the experiment time (Fig. 2 ). Therefore, it can be concluded that fluorescent hybrid nanogel possesses excellent stability performance against light exposure, which is of great importance in bioimaging application.

Fig. 3 represents the FTIR spectra of chitosan, folate, and FA-CS conjugate to clarify the chemical structure of FA-CS and confirms the presence of amide-bond as proof of conjugation. Pure CS represents characteristic peaks at 3439, 2924, 2853, 1607, 1117, and 1039 cm−1. The peaks at about 1117 and 1039 cm−1 clearly indicate the presence of C-O-C and CC ring vibrations. The major peak at 1607.27 cm−1 shows free NH bending to the chitosan structure. The stretching vibrations of the OH bond of chitosan polymer was found at 3439.13 cm−1 while stretching vibrations of CH (carboxylic group) were observed at 2924.65 cm−1. FTIR spectra of FA shows characteristic peaks at 1483, 1606, 1640, 1694, and 3414 cm−1. The characterization absorption band at 3414 cm−1 is related to the -O-H and –C-O stretching of alcohol and phenol in the structure. Also, the stretching vibration of CO from (carboxylic acid) appeared at 1694 cm−1 and the peak at 1640 cm−1 indicated CO stretching of –CONH2 in the folic acid structure. Two peaks at about 1606 and 1483 cm−1 were corresponded to the phenyl and Pterin rings. FTIR spectra of FA-CS conjugates exhibits characteristic peaks at 1527, 1649, 2884, 3280, 3339, and 3438 cm −1. The characteristic absorption bands at 3438, 3339, and 3280 cm−1 clearly reveals –O-H and an extra NH stretching vibration in comparison with the chitosan diagram. In addition, another significant difference is a peak at 1649 cm−1 indicating CO (amide), which may be due to the formation of an amide bond between the amino group of chitosan and the carboxyl group of folic acid. As expected, the peak at 1694 cm−1 was disappeared in the FA-CS conjugate indicating the prosperous conjugation of FA with CS.

Definite morphology and size of as-prepared FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles were determined by electron microscopy. Typical TEM image indicated uniform and quasispherical shape of FA-CS (CD) hybrid nanogel with an average particle size of 60 nm. Likewise, the FESEM micrograph is in accordance with the TEM image. The size of the nanoparticle is actually between ideal ranges of the practical cargo-delivery (Fig. 4a, b).

Average hydrodynamic size, polydispersity, and zeta potential of the aqueous suspension of hybrid nanogel were measured by DLS as shown in Table 1 . The size distribution diagram of FA-CS (CD) and FA-CS (CD/AntimiR-21) have also reported in Fig. 5 a, b. The obtained result revealed that FA-CS(CD) fluorescent nanocarrier had a hydrodynamic size of 122.8 ± 42.71 nm, compared to FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanocomplexes which had a hydrodynamic size of 104.9 ± 70.68 nm. Moreover, the zeta potential of the two mentioned particles was +23.6 and + 14.0, respectively. The zeta potential decreased after antimiR-21 loading based on the neutralization of NH3 groups of chitosan with phosphate groups of antimiR-21 indicating its successful association with the particles. Moreover, expectedly the slight decline in particle size after antimiR-21 loading could be due to the strong Coulomb or electrostatic interactions formed between negatively charged antmiR-21 with cationic polymer leading to a more condensed and smaller web structure of chitosan [35,36]. The DLS measurement is based on the swollen state of the nanoparticles in the aqueous medium, while SEM studies the collapse forms of the nanoparticles so that the size distribution obtained from the DLS technique is 3 to 4 times larger than SEM images [37]. Noteworthy, particle size and surface zeta potential are among the critical factors in determining nanocarrier efficiency. The small size of nanoparticles has provided easier leakage into the tumor site leading to efficient passive targeting. Moreover, the high positively charged surface of nanoparticles not only is responsible for their stability through electrostatic repulsion between particles, but also could enhance their endocytosis via great interactions with the electronegative cell surface [38,39].

The capability of nanoparticles in loading oligonucleotides is one of the important factors considered in selecting a gene delivery carrier. The obtained efficiency of antimiR-21 loading for both CS(CD) and FA-CS(CD) was 80–90% demonstrating that the folate targeting ligand has no interference with oligonucleotide encapsulation. In comparison with the study performed by Yang et al. [23] the loading efficiency in this study has decreased slightly, which may be due to the presence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding efficiency of antimiR-21 with fluorescent nanocarrier
ce:section.ce:para: Further examination of FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid formation was carried out by agarose gel electrophoresis at different weight ratios of NPs/antimiR. As the NPs/antimiR-21 weight ratio increased, the migration of antimiR-21 in the gel slowed down, indicating that FA-CS successfully bounded to antimiR-21 by neutralizing its charge. Due to the absence of free antimiR-21 bond, the complete association of antimiR-21 with nanoparticles was observed in NP/antimiR-21 ratios greater than 1:75. Fig. 6 shows that all negatively charged antisense oligonucleotides were completely trapped in the FA-CS(CD) nanoparticles.

As an effective gene carrier, nanocarriers must be capable of condensing nucleic acids for further protection against nuclease degradation. For this purpose, a serum protection test was accomplished for naked antimiR-21 and FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel by incubation in RPMI-1640 cell culture medium containing 10% FBS. The result was verified through a gel retardation assay. Degradation of the naked antimiR-21 began immediately after treatment due to mixing with serum. Subsequently, most of antimiRs degraded after 6 h of incubation and could not be detected in the agarose gel anymore. In contrast, a bright and dense band was observed in the gel even after incubation for 24 h (no antimiR signs were visualized at 48 h), indicating that the FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) complex effectively protected antimiR from serum degradation. However, the bands were not as strong as the untreated free antimiR-21 due to the possibility of antimiR-21 remaining within the complex (Fig. 7 ). Accordingly, it is confirmed that the FA–CS(CD) nanoparticles could be an excellent vector for gene delivery.

The stability profile of FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel during storage is of great importance as well. These data give a remarkable insight into optimum media and temperature for the synthesized NPs to increase their practical application. The stability of NPs was measured through following the mean particle size changes after 15 days of storage in distilled water at 4 °C and also at ambient temperature. In order to evaluate the stability level of NPs in a suspended medium, deionized distilled water was used, which is widely preferred according to previous reports [40,41].

NPs stored at ambient temperature demonstrated a remarkable increase in size after a certain period of storage (Fig. 8 ). This significant increase in the size of the NPs appears to be associated with aggregation. On the other hand, size of the NPs stored at 4 °C, remained quite constant up to 15 days. The findings demonstrated that nanoparticle's aggregation and precipitation are more likely to happen at room temperature in comparison with 4 °C, which is probably related to the slower kinetic and thermodynamic motion of NPs in lower temperatures. Thus, it would be optimal status for the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of in vitro cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging application and targeting potential of FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel were examined based on the great fluorescence properties of the CDs, which is associated with nanocarriers. After 4 h of incubation of NPs with SKOV3 cells, fluorescence images of the cells were acquired using fluorescence microscopy and confirmed that nanoparticle represents the potential of simultaneous visualization and cell targeting. Fluorescence microscopy demonstrated that slight amounts of CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs were internalized in SKOV3 cells according to the weak fluorescence signals. While stronger fluorescence intensities were observed in the same cells incubated with FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs, that indicates higher labeling efficiency associated with folate receptors targeting (Fig. 9a). These qualitative results on cell uptake strongly confirm the targeting ability of the FA-CS(CD) on SKOV3 cells.

In addition, NIH3T3 cells as folate receptor-negative control cells were also imaged for further investigation of the targeting efficiency of folate conjugated nanoparticles. The result clearly reveals a decrease in nanoparticle uptake in NIH3T3 cells compared to SKOV3 cells under the same condition. This comparison demonstrated that the CD emission intensity from NIH3T3 was not as intense as that of SKOV3 cells, that confirmed the effective targeting of FA-CS(CD) NPs to the cells over-expressing folate receptor. Meanwhile, CDs fluorescence intensity was almost the same even if the NPs applied in Fig. 9b were not folate targeted. This is due to folate receptor deficiency on NIH3T3 cell surface. Thus, the uptake rate of both CS(CD/antimiR-21) or FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs by NIH3T3 cells is the same.

Quantitative analysis of the photoluminescence intensity profile verified that the FA-CS (CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel uptake by SKOV3 cells was almost 2-fold higher than those treated with CS(CD/antimiR-21) NPs. Additionally, FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) hybrid nanogel uptake by SKOV3 cells was also higher than NIH3T3 cells incubated with both NPs (Fig. 9c). Therefore, it confirmed the presence of folate on the surface of fluorescent nanocarriers that provides further support for our theory and design. Moreover, Fig. 9c shows the fluorescent signal intensity of the reflectance images obtained from the positive and negative cell lines treated with CS(CD/antimiR) and FA-CS(CD/antimiR) nanogels.

The safety of fluorescent nanocarriers for therapeutic and diagnostic applications remains challenging. Cellular cytotoxicity of CS(CD), FA-CS(CD) and antimiR-21 loaded nanoparticles were evaluated using different concentrations against SKOV3 cells illuminating the biocompatibility and safe concentration for delivery. Clearly, CS(CD), FA-CS(CD) and FA–CS(CD/antimiR-21) nanoparticles barely affected cell viability (about 10–15% cell viability loss) in the concentration up to 160 μg/mL after 48 h incubation (Fig. 10 ), indicating low cytotoxicity of NPs for efficient delivery application.

In summary, a biocompatible, novel CD/antimiR co-delivered, folate-targeted chitosan hybrid nanogel was designed through a self-assembly method for tumor cell-specific fluorescence imaging and successful delivery of antisense oligonucleotides. The combination of four functional agents in FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NP not only maintains the individual features of each constituent but also demonstrates cooperative features for simultaneous targeting, imaging, and delivery purposes. According to characterization studies, while nanoparticles possessed desirable morphology and nano-sized structure, embedded CDs brought about a stable fluorescence effect. MTT assay confirmed the low toxicity of fluorescent nanocarrier which makes it reliable for safe application. Moreover, in vitro cellular imaging study showed enhanced uptake of folic acid conjugated NPs compared to folate-free NPs in SKOV3 cancer cells regarding a greater intensity of fluorescence signals. These data indicated that FA-CS(CD/antimiR-21) NP could provide a promising platform for co-delivery of CDs and antimiR-21 capable of bioimaging and targeted delivery of therapeutics to folate receptor over-expressing cancer cells.
All authors contributed equally to this work.

All persons who meet authorship criteria are listed as authors, and all authors certify that they have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for the content, including participation in the concept, design, writing, or revision of the manuscript. Furthermore, each author certifies that this manuscript has not been and will not be submitted to or published in any other publication before its appearance in the Reactive and Functional Polymers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran (Grant No. 59018).
